name: Visible label is part of accessible name
description: This rule checks that interactive elements labeled through content have their visible label as part of their accessible name.
applicability_text:
This rule applies to any element for which all the following is true:

- The element has a [semantic role][] that is a [widget][widget role] that [supports name from content][]; and
- The element has [visible text content][]; and
- The element has an `aria-label` or `aria-labelledby` attribute.
expectations: For each target element, all [text nodes][] in the [visible text content][] [match characters][] and are contained within the [accessible name][] of this target element, except for characters in the [text nodes][] used to express [non-text content][]. Leading and trailing [whitespace][] and difference in case sensitivity should be ignored.

examples.name: Passed Example 1
examples.description: This link has [visible][] text that matches the [accessible name][].

examples.name: Passed Example 2
examples.description: This link has [visible][] text that, ignoring trailing whitespace, matches the [accessible name][].

examples.name: Passed Example 3
examples.description: This link has [visible][] text that, ignoring case, matches the [accessible name][].

examples.name: Passed Example 4
examples.description: This button has [visible][] text that is contained within the [accessible name][].
examples.code: <button aria-label="Next Page in the list">Next Page</button>

examples.name: Passed Example 5
examples.description: This button has [visible][] text that does not need to be contained within the [accessible name][], because the "x" text node is [non-text content][]. Note: this would need to meet SC 1.1.1 Non text content.

examples.name: Passed Example 6
examples.description: This `button` element has the text "search" rendered as an magnifying glass icon by the font. Because the text is rendered as [non-text content][], the text does not need to be contained within the [accessible name][].

examples.name: Failed Example 1
examples.description: This link has [visible][] text that is different from the [accessible name][].

examples.name: Failed Example 2
examples.description: This button has [visible][] text that is only partially contained within the [accessible name][].

examples.name: Failed Example 3
examples.description: This link has [visible][] text with mathematical symbols, that does not match the [accessible name][] because the mathematical symbols were written out in the accessible name. This is [explicitly mentioned in WCAG](https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG22/Understanding/label-in-name#mathematical-expressions-and-formulae).
examples.code: Proof of 2×2=4

examples.name: Failed Example 4
examples.description: This link has [visible][] text does not match the [accessible name][] because there is a hyphen in the accessible name.

examples.name: Failed Example 5
examples.description: This link has [visible][] text does not match the [accessible name][] because there are extra spaces in the accessible name.

examples.name: Inapplicable Example 1
examples.description: This `nav` is not a widget, so the [visible][] text does not need to match the [accessible name][].

examples.name: Inapplicable Example 2
examples.description: This email text field does not need to have its [visible][] text match the [accessible name][]. The content of a textfield shows its value instead of its label; it does not [support name from content][supports name from content]. The label is usually adjacent to the textfield instead.

examples.name: Inapplicable Example 3
examples.description: This `div` element does not have a widget role, so the [visible][] text does not need to match the [accessible name][].

examples.name: Inapplicable Example 4
examples.description: This link has no [visible text content][].

background:
This rule applies to elements with a [widget role][] that [support name from content][supports name from content]. This includes the following: `button`, `checkbox`, `gridcell`, `link`, `menuitem`, `menuitemcheckbox`, `menuitemradio`, `option`, `radio`, `searchbox`, `switch`, `tab`, `treeitem`.

The understanding document of [2.5.3 Label in Name][understand253] use the term "symbolic text characters" to refer to a type of [non-text content][] that uses text characters as symbols, such as using "x" to mean "close". This rule considers them as "characters expressing non-text content". Unicode emojis are another example of characters expressing non-text content, although these are not "symbolic text characters".
assumptions: This rule assumes that all resources needed for rendering the page are properly loaded. Checking if resources are missing is out of the scope of rules. Missing resources may be rendered as text (for example, missing `img` are rendered as their `alt` attribute).
accessibility_support: Implementation of [Presentational Roles Conflict Resolution][] varies from one browser or assistive technology to another. Depending on this, some elements can have one of the applicable [semantic roles][semantic role] and fail this rule with some technology but users of other technologies would not experience any accessibility issue.
